triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis is the first step in the breakdown of glucose to extract energy for cellular metabolism.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis begins with the six-carbon ring-shaped structure of a single glucose molecule and ends with two molecules of a three-carbon sugar called pyruvate.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis begins with glucose and produces two pyruvate molecules, four new ATP molecules, and two molecules of NADH.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis begins with glucose and produces two pyruvate molecules, four new ATP molecules, and two molecules of NADH.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The first half of glycolysis uses two ATP molecules in the phosphorylation of glucose, which is then split into two three-carbon molecules.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The continuation of the reaction depends upon the availability of the oxidized form of the electron carrier, NAD+.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm of both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

triples.head: glucose
triples.evidence: The other mechanism uses a group of integral proteins called GLUT proteins, also known as glucose transporter proteins.

triples.head: glucose
triples.evidence: Glucose enters heterotrophic cells in two ways. One method is through secondary active transport in which the transport takes place against the glucose concentration gradient.

triples.head: hexokinase
triples.evidence: The first step in glycolysis (Figure 7.8) is catalyzed by hexokinase, an enzyme with broad specificity that catalyzes the phosphorylation of six-carbon sugars.

triples.head: hexokinase
triples.evidence: Hexokinase phosphorylates glucose using ATP as the source of the phosphate, producing glucose-6-phosphate, a more reactive form of glucose.

triples.head: isomerase
triples.evidence: In the second step of glycolysis, an isomerase converts glucose-6-phosphate into one of its isomers, fructose-6-phosphate.

triples.head: phosphofructokinase
triples.evidence: The third step is the phosphorylation of fructose-6-phosphate, catalyzed by the enzyme phosphofructokinase.

triples.head: phosphofructokinase
triples.evidence: A second ATP molecule donates a high-energy phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate, producing fructose-1,6-bisphosphate.

triples.head: aldolase
triples.evidence: The fourth step in glycolysis employs an enzyme, aldolase, to cleave fructose-1,6-bisphosphate into two three-carbon isomers: dihydroxyacetone phosphate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

triples.head: isomerase
triples.evidence: In the fifth step, an isomerase transforms the dihydroxyacetone-phosphate into its isomer, glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

triples.head: glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate
triples.evidence: The sixth step in glycolysis (Figure 7.9) oxidizes the sugar (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate), extracting high-energy electrons, which are picked up by the electron carrier NAD+, producing NADH.

triples.head: phosphoglycerate kinase
triples.evidence: In the seventh step, catalyzed by phosphoglycerate kinase, 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate donates a high-energy phosphate to ADP, forming one molecule of ATP.

triples.head: mutase
triples.evidence: In the eighth step, the remaining phosphate group in 3-phosphoglycerate moves from the third carbon to the second carbon, producing 2-phosphoglycerate (an isomer of 3-phosphoglycerate).

triples.head: enolase
triples.evidence: Enolase catalyzes the ninth step. This enzyme causes 2-phosphoglycerate to lose water from its structure; this is a dehydration reaction, resulting in the formation of a double bond that increases the potential energy in the remaining phosphate bond and produces phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP).

triples.head: pyruvate kinase
triples.evidence: The last step in glycolysis is catalyzed by the enzyme pyruvate kinase and results in the production of a second ATP molecule by substrate-level phosphorylation and the compound pyruvic acid (or its salt form, pyruvate).

triples.head: pyruvate kinase
triples.evidence: The last step in glycolysis is catalyzed by the enzyme pyruvate kinase and results in the production of a second ATP molecule by substrate-level phosphorylation and the compound pyruvic acid (or its salt form, pyruvate).

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The process does not use oxygen directly and therefore is termed anaerobic.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm of both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm of both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: Glycolysis consists of two distinct phases. The first part of the glycolysis pathway traps the glucose molecule in the cell and uses energy to modify it so that the six-carbon sugar molecule can be split evenly into the two three-carbon molecules.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The second part of glycolysis extracts energy from the molecules and stores it in the form of ATP and NADH—remember: this is the reduced form of NAD.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The second part of glycolysis extracts energy from the molecules and stores it in the form of ATP and NADH—remember: this is the reduced form of NAD.

triples.head: glycolysis
triples.evidence: The second part of glycolysis extracts energy from the molecules and stores it in the form of ATP and NADH—remember: this is the reduced form of NAD.
